description: Comprehensive Hyperliquid Exporter v3 dashboard. Visualizes every metric family the exporter emits: sync state, HyperCore block production, HyperEVM, consensus (validator-only), validator set, P2P/network, LZ4 compression, node processes, disk, subsystem latency, critical messages, Tokio runtime, RocksDB, info probe, and exporter self-observability. Use the job/instance dropdowns to filter across nodes.

links.title: Per-validator detail
links.tooltip: Open the per-validator drilldown dashboard, scoped to a chosen validator address.

panels.description: Mirrors /readyz. 1 once every registered monitor goroutine has launched. Drops to 0 if a monitor fails to start.
panels.title: Exporter Ready

panels.description: hl-node process state (from /proc on Linux). 1 = process found, 0 = missing. Companion gauges (CPU/RSS/etc.) zero out on miss.
panels.title: hl-node Process

panels.description: hl-visor process state. hl-visor is the parent supervisor that downloads new hl-node binaries and spawns the child.
panels.title: hl-visor Process

panels.description: 1 = local hl-node commit hash matches the latest binary published at binaries.hyperliquid.xyz. 0 = upgrade pending. Checked every 30 minutes.
panels.title: hl-node Up-to-Date

panels.description: Active info-endpoint probe (POST :3001/info {type:meta}). 1 = 200 + non-empty body in last probe (15s tick). Requires --probe-info-endpoint; permanently 0 without it.
panels.title: Info Endpoint
Exporter Build
panels.description: Exporter build identity baked in at link time. version is the release tag, commit is the short git hash, go_version is the toolchain. Always exactly one row per scraped target.
Per-monitor liveness. last_tick is when each monitor last processed real data; large values mean the monitor is stalled. panics and errors are cumulative recoveries since exporter start.
panels.title: Monitor Health (age of last tick, panics, errors)

panels.description: Latest block height applied by hl-visor. This is the operator-facing 'where the node is' number.
panels.title: Visor Height

panels.description: Blocks applied since the current hl-visor instance started (height − initial_height). Resets to 0 on every restart.
panels.title: Blocks Applied This Run

panels.description: visor_height − freeze_abci_height. Operator can roll back at most this many blocks before hitting the replay floor.
panels.title: Blocks Above Freeze

panels.description: Age of the most recent visor state sample read by the exporter. > 60s suggests the sync state file has stopped updating; investigate hl-visor.
panels.title: Visor Sample Age

panels.description: Seconds since the most recent successful periodic ABCI snapshot (per status sentinel mtime). > 900s = snapshot pipeline stalled.
panels.title: Last Snapshot Age

panels.description: consensus_time − wall_clock_time. ~+20s is normal (block timestamps decided at consensus, observed after propagation). Sustained |value| > 60s suggests NTP drift or sync issue.
panels.title: Consensus−Wall Skew

panels.description: Block height applied by hl-visor over time. A flat line = node not advancing; a jump = catch-up; a drop = restart resetting initial_height (height itself only drops on rewind).
panels.title: Visor Height Over Time

panels.description:
Two related sync signals. consensus−wall (left axis) is the gap between the chain's consensus timestamp on the latest block and this node's local wall-clock at the moment the visor recorded the sample. POSITIVE = chain consensus clock is ahead of local (normal: ~+20s, because blocks are timestamped at consensus and observed locally after propagation). NEGATIVE = local clock has drifted ahead (NTP problem).

reference_lag (same axis when populated) is the gap to a configured reference node — only meaningful when the operator set one. Empty otherwise.
panels.title: Sync Skew & Reference Lag

panels.description: Replay floor (height the visor can roll back to) and a scheduled freeze height if set (0 = no scheduled freeze). Scheduled freezes are how chain upgrades are coordinated.
panels.title: Freeze Floors

panels.description: Periodic ABCI snapshot pipeline. last_height is the most recent snapshot taken, known_count is how many sentinels are retained. The pair lets you tell 'snapshot pipeline alive but height stuck' from 'pipeline dead'.
panels.title: Snapshot Pipeline

panels.description: Latest HyperCore block height observed from block_times files. This is the core L1 height (distinct from HyperEVM).
panels.title: Core Block Height

panels.description: Unix timestamp of the latest core block as recorded by the node (the chain's consensus time, not local wall clock).
panels.title: Latest Block Time (UTC)

panels.description: Most recent consensus round processed by the replica monitor. Requires --replica-metrics + hl-node --replica-cmds-style actions-and-responses.
panels.title: Last Processed Round

panels.description: Rate of blocks processed by the replica monitor over the last 5 min. Should track the chain's ~5 blocks/sec mainnet cadence.
panels.title: Blocks/sec (5m avg)

panels.description: Time between consecutive blocks (block_time delta). p50 is the typical cadence; p95/p99 reveal stalls. Labeled by state_type (fast/slow) when both ABCI tiers are tracked.
panels.title: Block Time Percentiles

panels.description: How long the node spent applying each committed block (CPU-bound execution time). Different from Block Time Percentiles, which is the wall-clock interval BETWEEN blocks — apply duration is the actual work done per block. p50 ≈ healthy CPU pace; p99 climbing = node falling behind on heavy blocks.
panels.title: Block Apply Duration Percentiles

panels.description: Per-second rate of HyperCore actions (orders, cancels, transfers, …) by action type. Sum across types ≈ chain TPS. Requires --replica-metrics.
panels.title: Core TX Rate by Type

panels.description: Per-second rate of individual operations (one batchModify can contain N modifies; this counts the N, not the 1). Categories: trading / transfer / settings / governance / rewards / evm / other.
panels.title: Core Operations Rate by Category

panels.description: Distribution of transaction count per block. Heatmap shows how bursty load is — a thin band at low values = steady, wide spread = load spikes.
panels.title: Transactions Per Block (heatmap)

panels.description: Total orders per second across all action types that produce orders (order + twapOrder). Useful when you want trading-volume signal isolated from ops noise.
panels.title: Orders Per Second

panels.description: Latest HyperEVM block height. The EVM chain runs alongside HyperCore with its own block stream.
panels.title: EVM Block Height

panels.description: Unix timestamp of the latest EVM block.
panels.title: Latest EVM Block Time

panels.description: Current EVM gas utilization (gas_used / gas_limit) for the latest block. Sustained >0.8 means EVM is throughput-bound.
panels.title: EVM Gas Utilization

panels.description: Total number of EVM accounts (from ABCI state msgpack). Monotonically growing; rate gives 'new accounts per day'. 60s startup delay + 5s polls.
panels.title: EVM Account Count

panels.description: Time between consecutive EVM blocks. Mainnet EVM cadence is ~1s; spread reveals load impact.
panels.title: EVM Block Time Percentiles

panels.description: EIP-1559 base fee (network demand) and the max priority fee observed in the latest block (auction premium). Both in gwei; per block_type when --evm-metrics is on.
panels.title: EVM Base Fee & Max Priority Fee (gwei)

panels.description: Gas used vs gas limit per block. The gap between the two = how much headroom remains. Limit jumps at the 3M / 30M tiers.
panels.title: EVM Gas Used vs Limit

panels.description: EVM transactions per second by transaction type (Legacy / Eip1559 / Eip2930 / …). Sum approximates EVM TPS.
panels.title: EVM TX Rate by Type

panels.description: Fast tier vs slow tier EVM DB checkpoint heights, and the slow-vs-fast lag. Sustained lag > 100 blocks = slow tier is falling behind, RPC reads of historical state will be incorrect.
panels.title: EVM DB Checkpoints (fast/slow)

panels.description: Cumulative count of EVM contract creations (CREATE / CREATE2 transactions detected by empty/zero 'to' field).
panels.title: EVM Contract Creations/sec

panels.description: Last block whose gas limit was ≥30M. Useful as a tripwire — the 30M tier indicates a chain-upgrade-driven large block, rare in normal operation.
panels.title: Last High-Gas Block

panels.description: Top contracts by transaction count. Requires --contract-metrics. Labels resolved via hyperscan.com API at first sight; up to --contract-metrics-limit unique addresses tracked.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (contract_name, contract_address, symbol, type) (rate(hl_evm_contract_tx_total{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}[5m])))

panels.title: Top EVM Contracts (tx/sec, 5m)

panels.description: Current HotStuff consensus round, parsed from consensus log block messages. Validator nodes only — non-validator peers don't have this log.
panels.title: Current Consensus Round

panels.description: Average rounds needed to produce a block. 1.0 = perfect (every round commits). >1 = view changes / TC fallbacks happening.
panels.title: Rounds / Block (avg)

panels.description: Total QC signatures observed per second across all validators (this node's view). Healthy network = ~5 × validator_count / sec (one QC per block × ~5 blocks/sec).
panels.title: QC Signatures/sec

panels.description: Rate of TC (Timeout Certificate) blocks — view-change events where the previous round timed out. >0.01 sustained means recurring view changes.
panels.title: TC Blocks/sec

panels.description: Per-validator QC participation rate (% of last 100 blocks whose QC included their signature). Healthy validators sit near 100%; <90% indicates the validator is missing rounds.
panels.title: QC Participation Rate (per validator)

panels.description:
Round-trip time distribution for heartbeat→heartbeat-ack exchanges. Y-axis = milliseconds.

Validator nodes periodically send Heartbeat messages to peers; each peer replies with HeartbeatAck. The delay between send and receive is plotted as p50/p95/p99 percentiles.

Healthy: p95 well under 50ms, p99 under 200ms. Sustained spikes = network problems between this validator and the rest of the active set.
panels.title: Heartbeat Ack Delay Percentiles

panels.description:
Distribution of signer counts per QC/TC. Y-axis = number of validators whose signatures appeared on a single QC (or whose timeout votes formed a single TC).

For a healthy validator set: QC sizes should hover near 2/3 of the active set (the BFT supermajority threshold). TC sizes spike during view-change events. A QC distribution drifting LOW = consensus is barely reaching quorum each round, a precursor to outages.
panels.title: QC / TC Signer Counts

panels.description: Heartbeats sent and acks received per second. Mismatches imply the local validator is sending but peers aren't acknowledging (or vice versa).
panels.title: Heartbeat Send/Ack Rate

panels.description: Current disconnected validator pairs from status logs. Each row is (validator, peer) showing the local validator's view of who's unreachable. Only disconnected pairs appear (value=0); reconnects remove the series.
panels.title: Disconnected Validator Pairs

panels.description: Per-validator network latency from validator_latency_ema files. Lower = faster path between this node and the target validator. Validator nodes only.
panels.title: Validator Latency EMA (top 15)

panels.description: Total stake across the network (sum of all validators). From the public api.hyperliquid.xyz validator summaries endpoint, polled every 5 min.
panels.title: Total Stake (HYPE)

panels.description: Stake of currently-active validators (the set producing blocks). Should be most of total_stake on a healthy chain.
panels.title: Active Stake (HYPE)

panels.description: Stake of validators currently jailed (sanctioned for misbehavior). Sustained non-zero is normal; large jumps indicate slashing events.
panels.title: Jailed Stake (HYPE)

panels.description: Stake of validators that exist but aren't currently active (set membership churn).
panels.title: Inactive Stake (HYPE)

panels.description: Count of all validators in the network (active + inactive + jailed).
panels.title: Validator Count

panels.description: Stake of currently un-jailed validators (eligible for consensus).
panels.title: Not-Jailed Stake (HYPE)

panels.description: Top 30 validators ranked by current stake. X-axis = stake in HYPE (raw value / 1e8). The longer the bar, the bigger the validator. Gradient colour shows magnitude only; click any bar to drill into the per-validator detail dashboard.
panels.title: Top 30 Validators by Stake (HYPE)

panels.description:
Per-validator block-share divided by stake-share over the last 1h. 1.0 means this validator proposed in exact proportion to its stake. Below 0.9 is under-producing (red); above 1.1 is over-producing (blue). Bar is capped at 2.0 for readability — short-window spikes (a small validator winning two slots in a row) can produce numerical values much higher; check the displayed number on the right of the bar for the actual ratio.

Validators with zero proposals in the last hour don't appear here at all.

targets.expr: sort_desc(( sum by (validator) (rate(hl_consensus_proposer_count_total{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}[1h]))   / scalar(sum(rate(hl_consensus_proposer_count_total{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}[1h]))) ) / on(validator) group_left(name) ( max by (validator, name) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"})   / scalar(sum(hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"})) ))

panels.title: Proposer Fairness Ratio (1.0 = fair share, 1h)

panels.description:
Currently ESTABLISHED TCP connections per hl-node listening port, from /proc/net/tcp. Y-axis = connection count.

Port reference (Hyperliquid mainnet):
  4001 — primary gossip (most traffic; biggest peer count here)
  4002 — secondary gossip (smaller)
  3001 — --serve-info (HTTP API for /info; <10 connections normal)
  3999 — --serve-eth-rpc (allowlist-filtered; near-zero)
panels.title: ESTABLISHED TCP Connections by Port

panels.description:
/proc/net/tcp socket-state counts for hl-node's secondary gossip port (4002), stacked. Y-axis = number of sockets in that state. Watch:
  • ESTABLISHED — live peer connections (the real connected-peer count)
  • TIME_WAIT — recently-closed sockets waiting out 2×MSL (~60s)
  • SYN_RECV — handshake in progress

On testnet/mainnet validators we've observed transient TIME_WAIT spikes to 30+ on this port during peer-set churn — normal. Sustained TIME_WAIT > 50 for >10 min = peer-side issues; > 100 for >10 min = something broken in the local network path.
panels.title: Port 4002 TCP States (peer churn)

panels.description: Top-16 inbound peers by hl-node's tcp_traffic file. Values are hl-node's per-30s normalized throughput (units undocumented; treat as comparative rate). ip="other" rolls up the long tail.
panels.title: Peer Traffic IN (top-16 + other)

panels.description: Same as IN but outbound. Useful to see if this node is acting as a hub for downstream peers.
panels.title: Peer Traffic OUT (top-16 + other)

panels.description:
**What is a parent peer?** On a non-validator node, the 'parent peer' is the inbound peer delivering the most block-data bytes in the latest tcp_traffic sample — effectively this node's upstream source of chain data. hl-node doesn't explicitly elect a parent; the exporter identifies it as the peer with the highest inbound bandwidth.

**Why it matters:** lose the parent and the node likely stops syncing until it finds a new one. Rapid parent switches (more than a few per minute) usually indicate upstream instability or this node bridging multiple peers of similar weight.

**Columns:** IP = current parent's address. Tenure = how long they've held the role since the exporter last observed a switch. Switches = cumulative count of parent changes since exporter start.
panels.title: Parent Peer

panels.description:
Gossip-connection events per second, stacked by event_type. Y-axis = events/sec.

Event types:
  • verified_gossip_rpc — healthy peer RPC (baseline activity)
  • handle_stream_connection — new inbound TCP stream
  • got_tcp_greeting — peer sent initial greeting
  • performing_checks_on_stream — verifying peer identity
  • error_checking_connection — peer verification failed (peer churn / malicious traffic)
  • rejecting_gossip_stream_max_peers_reached — peer cap reached — OPERATOR-ACTIONABLE (raise n_gossip_peers if you want more peers)
panels.title: Gossip Connection Events/sec

panels.description: Non-validator peers connected (verified vs unverified). Verified peers count toward consensus participation. Total = the visible non-val peer count.
panels.title: Non-Validator Peer Connections

panels.description:
Distinct peers seen in the most recent tcp_traffic sample, per direction (left axis), plus the age of that sample in seconds (right axis).

Healthy: peer counts in the dozens-to-hundreds, sample age <60s. Sample age growing without bound = the tcp_traffic file stopped being written (hl-node trouble).
panels.title: Peer Count & Sample Age

panels.description: Global LZ4 compression ratio (compressed÷uncompressed). Healthy peer protocol ≈ 0.6 (40% savings). Drift toward 1.0 = uncompressible payloads (attack or regression).
panels.title: LZ4 Global Ratio

panels.description: Cumulative compressed bytes & packets across all peers since hl-node start. Both gauges (reset on hl-node restart). Useful to see traffic-volume rate of change.
panels.title: LZ4 Global Volume (cumulative gauges)

panels.description:
Per-peer LZ4 compression ratio: bytes-after / bytes-before, so 0.62 = compressed to 62% (38% savings). Y-axis = compression ratio (0.0-1.0).

  • ~0.5-0.7 — healthy: peer traffic compresses well (block bytes are structured, lots of repeated hashes/addresses).
  • Climbing toward 1.0 — peer is sending uncompressible data. Could be attack traffic injecting already-compressed payloads, or a peer-side regression. Worth investigating that IP.
  • 'other' band is the long-tail rollup of peers below the top-N cutoff.
panels.title: Per-Peer LZ4 Ratio

panels.description: Per-direction-per-port LZ4 codec latency at p95. Y = seconds spent in lz4 encode/decode per peer payload. UNDOCUMENTED QUIRK observed on Hyperliquid: port 4002 traffic latency runs ~10x HIGHER than 4001 even on a healthy node. Don't read 4002 latency in isolation as a problem signal; compare against 4001 and look for sustained RATIO change rather than absolute value.
panels.title: LZ4 Latency p95 by Direction/Port

panels.description: Per-second CPU consumption per process. The Y-axis is cores-equivalent: 0.18 = using 18% of one logical core; 2.5 = using 2.5 cores in parallel. Computed from rate(/proc/PID/stat utime+stime). On a busy validator hl-node typically sits between 0.2 and 2.0 cores depending on chain load; sustained values approaching the number of CPUs on the box = saturation.
panels.title: Process CPU Use (cores)

panels.description: Resident set size (VmRSS) of each process. Steady growth = memory leak suspect; sudden drop = restart (cross-check process_start_time).
panels.title: Process RSS Memory

panels.description: OS-level thread count per process. Both processes use heavy threading; jumps may indicate runtime task spawning or stuck threads.
panels.title: Process Threads

panels.description: Open file descriptors per process. hl-node holds many sockets + DB files; sustained growth or values near the soft ulimit = leak.
panels.title: Process Open FDs

panels.description: Unix timestamp of each process's start. Steps upward = restart events; horizontal segments = process stable. Useful to count restarts via changes().
panels.title: Process Start Time

panels.description: Filesystem-observed hl-node runs (count of data/replica_cmds/<run_timestamp>/ subdirs) and the newest run's start time. Independent restart-count signal from /proc — works in containers too.
panels.title: Filesystem-Observed Runs

panels.description: Filesystem fill ratio for the partition holding NODE_HOME. >85% = imminent crash risk; node will silently break when disk fills.
panels.title: Disk Used %

panels.description: NODE_HOME tree size (sum of regular file sizes from a recursive walk). Steady growth = node operating normally; sudden jumps may need investigation.
panels.title: Disk Used vs Total

panels.description: Bytes still available on the filesystem. Drop to single-digit GBs = emergency.
panels.title: Disk Free

panels.description: Bytes consumed by each tracked NODE_HOME subdir. RocksDBs (db_hub/Rpc, evm_db_hub_slow) and replica_cmds dominate; sudden growth in a specific subdir tells you what to clean up.
panels.title: NODE_HOME Subdirectory Sizes

panels.description:
p95 latency for each tracked hl-node subsystem. Y = seconds.

Most operator-actionable rows:
  • node_fast_begin_block_to_commit — main block-production path
  • node_fast_block_duration — total time per block
  • bucket_guard — per-block accounting; spikes = expensive blocks
  • tokio_scheduled_observer_fast — async runtime stall canary
panels.title: Subsystem p95 Latency

panels.description: Max observed latency per subsystem in the sample window. p95 smooths over outliers; max catches them. A subsystem with p95=50ms but max=2s has occasional 2-second spikes worth investigating, even if the typical operation is fast.
panels.title: Subsystem Max Latency

panels.description: Fraction of wall-clock time each subsystem spent doing work. Near 1.0 = saturated; near 0 = idle.
panels.title: Subsystem Work Fraction

panels.description: Mean per-sample latency per subsystem (from the n-buffer sliding window). The companion p95 panel above shows the tail; this one shows the typical case. If MEAN is rising while p95 is flat = every operation is slower (broad slowdown). If MEAN is flat while p95 is rising = a few outlier operations are slow (tail latency).
panels.title: Subsystem Mean Latency

panels.description: Per-step p95 latency for bucket_guard sub-steps (begin_block, distribute_funding, build_evm_block, etc.). These are the finer-grained timings inside the block production loop.
panels.title: bucket_guard p95 by Step

panels.description: Per-step max latency for bucket_guard. Spikes here = tail-latency outliers; correlate with crit_msg / log_lines around the same timestamp.
panels.title: bucket_guard Max by Step

panels.description: Mean and work_fraction for bucket_guard steps. work_fraction near 1 = step is the bottleneck.
panels.title: bucket_guard work_fraction by Step

panels.description: Full quantile spectrum for tcp_lz4 per direction/port. Useful to compare in vs out, and 4001 vs 4002 (4002 is known-slower).
panels.title: tcp_lz4 Latency Spectrum

panels.description: Cumulative count of bug! events since the source process started. Any non-zero value = page-someone severity. Resets on hl-node restart (gauge by design).
panels.title: Bugs (page severity)

panels.description: Cumulative count of crit! events. Alert-tier; sustained rate > 0 means something is going wrong now. Resets on source restart.
panels.title: Crits (alert tier)

panels.description: Distinct (file, line) source-code call sites that have emitted at least one bug or crit. Growth = a NEW crit type just appeared (vs an existing one firing repeatedly).
panels.title: Distinct Crit Locations

panels.description: Unix timestamp the current critical-message counters started accumulating. Equals the source process start. A jump here = restart; if accompanied by counter resets, that's the visible signature.
panels.title: Crit Counter Reset Time

panels.description: Per-source-location crit detail (requires --extended-metrics; reads /tmp/crit_msg_latest_stats/hl-node.json). file:line identifies the exact source line in hl-node that emitted the crit. Last-seen lets you spot recurring vs old.
panels.title: Crit Locations Detail

panels.description: Slow polls per second per async task. A poll is 'slow' when it exceeds the runtime's threshold. Sustained rate on most tasks = async runtime starved. NOTE: nv_stream_forward_client_blocks naturally has hundreds — alert on the OTHER tasks.
panels.title: Tokio Slow Polls/sec

panels.description: Long-delay events per second — task waited too long to be polled after becoming ready. Indicates scheduler contention.
panels.title: Tokio Long Delays/sec

panels.description: Cumulative seconds spent polling each task. Rate of change = how busy that task is. Compare against idle_seconds to see busy-vs-waiting ratio.
panels.title: Tokio Poll-Seconds Rate (CPU share)

panels.description: Dropped tasks (panicked or cancelled). Any growth = a task in the runtime died. Cross-reference with crit_msg around the same timestamp.
panels.title: Tokio Dropped Tasks (cumulative)

panels.description: Lines in today's log file by stream (infra/trade) and level (error/warn). Modeled as gauge so the day-file rotation cleanly zeroes at UTC midnight. Requires --extended-metrics.
panels.title: Log Lines Today (per stream/level)

panels.description: Today's log file size in bytes. Healthy node has both lines and bytes very low; anything growing fast points to a noisy bug.
panels.title: Log Bytes Today

panels.description: Current public IP advertised by hl-node (file rewritten every ~13 min as heartbeat). Showing the IP as a label so changes are visible. Requires --extended-metrics.
panels.title: Current Public IP

panels.description: Age of last_known_public_ip.json. Should refresh every ~13min while hl-node runs. >30min suggests the IP heartbeat path is broken.
panels.title: Public IP Heartbeat Age

panels.description: Cumulative count of public-IP changes observed since exporter start. Non-zero = NAT/cloud-instance IP flipped (or operator moved the node).
panels.title: Public IP Changes

panels.description: Age (now − mtime) of operator-edited config files under file_mod_time_tracker/. Tells you 'when did the operator last touch this'. Useful in postmortems.
panels.title: Operator Config File Ages

panels.description: Count of .sst files per RocksDB instance (LSM tree size). Steady or oscillating = compaction healthy. Sustained growth without compaction = compaction stuck.
panels.title: RocksDB SST File Count

panels.description: Current block-cache usage per RocksDB, from the LOG file's DUMPING STATS block. Approaches the configured cache size on a busy DB.
panels.title: RocksDB Block Cache Usage

panels.description:
RocksDB write-stall counts by reason. Y = cumulative count since hl-node started (resets on restart).

Most important reasons:
  • pending-compaction-bytes-stops — LSM tree falling behind on compaction; writes BLOCK until compaction catches up. The single most actionable stall reason.
  • l0-file-count-limit-stops — too many L0 SST files; same idea, different trigger.
  • memtable-limit-stops — memtable full, waiting on flush.
  • total-stops — sum of all hard-stop events.
  • *-delays — soft delays (writes throttled but not blocked).

All zero = healthy LSM. Sustained growth on any *-stops = RocksDB throughput problem; either disk too slow or compaction config wrong.
panels.title: RocksDB Write Stalls by Reason

panels.description: Total size of $NODE_HOME/tmp. Persistent growth = orphaned writes from past hl-node crashes (the shell.rs panic class). Cleanup is operator-side.
panels.title: Tmp Dir Total Bytes

panels.description: Files under $NODE_HOME/tmp older than 24h. Each one is an orphaned write candidate — typically a fu::write_string_to_file_tmp left behind by a crash.
panels.title: Stale Tmp Files (>24h)

panels.description: Files under tmp/shell_rs_out/. One sentinel per visor shell-exec; sustained growth past a few thousand means hl-visor's cleanup pass is broken.
panels.title: Pending Shell-Exec Sentinels

panels.description: Result of the most recent POST :3001/info {type:meta}. 1 = 200 + non-empty body. The only true 'live process serving requests' health probe.
panels.title: Probe State

panels.description: Age of the last successful probe. >30s with the 15s tick = probe failing now.
panels.title: Last Success Age

panels.description: Probe failures per second over the last 5m. Sustained >0 = the node's HTTP RPC is unhealthy or unreachable.
panels.title: Failures/sec

panels.description: p95 of probe latency (POST + response). Spikes here without an up-flap = node is slow but still serving.
panels.title: Probe Latency p95 (5m)

panels.description: Latency percentiles over time so spikes are visible in context.
panels.title: Info Probe Latency Percentiles

panels.description: Heatmap of probe-latency distribution. Brightness shifting up = systemic slowdown; isolated bright cells = transient spikes.
panels.title: Info Probe Latency Heatmap

panels.description: Exporter Go process memory. heap_inuse = live objects, sys = total memory acquired from OS, heap_idle = freed but not returned. Sustained heap_inuse growth = leak in the exporter itself.
panels.title: Exporter Memory

panels.description: Live goroutines + heap object count. Goroutines should be stable; growth = leak (likely a monitor not cancelling).
panels.title: Goroutines & Heap Objects

panels.description: hl-node's own version info (separate from the exporter build). Each tile shows one label of hl_software_version — commit hash (full 40-char git SHA) and date (ISO timestamp the binary was built). Updates every 30 min when the version monitor runs `hl-node --version` on a temp copy of the binary.
panels.title: hl-node Version (commit / date)

panels.description: Average difference between a block's round number and the QC round it carries. Healthy ≈ 1 (block N's QC certifies block N−1). >1 = QCs lag behind blocks, view-change-y network.
panels.title: QC Round Lag

panels.description:
Inbound throughput the current parent peer is delivering (last 30s sample, in the raw per-30s units hl-node uses). Pairs with the Parent Peer table — when this drops to ~0 the parent stopped feeding blocks even though it still holds the role (parent identity is set on bytes-in-last-sample, so once a sample shows a quiet peer at the top, we lock onto them until somebody else outpaces them in a later sample).

Sustained zero here = the upstream connection is dead; expect a parent-switch within a few sample windows.
panels.title: Parent Peer Throughput (current)

panels.description: Aggregate inbound vs outbound TCP throughput summed across all peers (top-N + 'other'). The cleanest 'how much data is this node moving' signal; doesn't suffer the per-peer flapping.
panels.title: Total P2P Throughput (in/out)

panels.description: Per-validator heartbeat status from status logs. status_type 'since_last_success' = seconds since last successful heartbeat ack (high = unreachable). 'last_ack_duration' = duration of the most recent ack. Validator-only.
panels.title: Validator Heartbeat Status (top 10 stragglers)

panels.description: Process virtual memory size (VmSize) alongside RSS for comparison. Large VmSize − RSS gap = lots of memory-mapped files (e.g. RocksDB SSTs) the kernel can swap out without harm.
panels.title: Process VmSize vs VmRSS

panels.description: Raw per-validator network latency (latest sample, not the EMA panel earlier). Spikes here that don't show on EMA = transient measurement glitches.
panels.title: Raw Validator Latency (top 15)

panels.description: Timeout votes sent per validator per second — the participation side of TC blocks. Top-10 validators by TC vote rate. Validators sending lots of TC votes are 'seeing' more timeouts than peers.
panels.title: TC Participation Rate (top 10 validators)

panels.description: Replica monitor self-health: how long the JSON parse takes per line (gauge, latest) and how far behind the file tail the monitor is (last_processed_round shows the latest round it has decoded).
panels.title: Replica Monitor Self-Health

panels.description: Subsystem latency percentiles other than p95: median + p90 + stddev. Useful for distribution shape: high stddev with low median = bursty.
panels.title: Subsystem Latency — median / p90 / stddev

panels.description: Cumulative sample count behind each subsystem's stats. Rate of change = how many samples the subsystem produced per second (its throughput, in invocations).
panels.title: Subsystem Sample Throughput

panels.description: Total polls per second per Tokio task (slow + fast). The 'how active' counterpart to slow_polls. Healthy: nv_stream_forward_client_blocks dominates; the rest stay low.
panels.title: Tokio Total Polls/sec

panels.description: rate() of cumulative tokio task idle time per wall-clock second. For a single-worker task, 1.0 = fully idle (always awaiting work). Tasks with many concurrent worker threads can have aggregate idle rates well above 1.0 — that is normal and means the worker pool has spare capacity. Pair with the Tokio Poll-Seconds panel: low poll + high idle = task healthy; high poll + low idle = task saturated; both low = task quiescent.
panels.title: Tokio Idle-Seconds Rate

panels.description: Percentile distribution of EVM base fee + max priority fee, computed across all observed blocks (not just the latest). p99 shifts up = fee spikes; p50 shifts up = sustained demand increase.
panels.title: EVM Fee Distribution Percentiles (gwei)

panels.description: High-gas (≥30M) blocks per second by threshold label. Almost always zero; sustained non-zero = the chain has shifted to producing bigger blocks routinely.
panels.title: EVM High-Gas-Limit Blocks/sec

panels.description: EVM transactions-per-block distribution as a heatmap. Brightness up + right = sustained high-tx blocks (genuine demand); isolated bright cells = bursty.
panels.title: EVM TX Per Block (heatmap)

panels.description: Distribution of EVM gas limits across blocks. Bimodal (~2M + ~30M) is the historical normal; new bands appearing = chain upgrade tier change.
panels.title: EVM Gas Limit Distribution (heatmap)

panels.description: Per-validator vote freshness: last_vote_round + seconds since the validator's last vote. Sortable by time_diff — large values mean the validator stopped voting recently. Validator-only.
panels.title: Validator Vote Freshness

panels.description: Active TCP-probe RTT to each known validator's gossip port. Spikes = network jitter to that peer. Requires --validator-rtt and validator profiles in ABCI state.
panels.title: Validator RTT (line chart, microseconds)

panels.description: Cumulative txs dropped by the validator's mempool/consensus pipeline since hl-node started. ANY non-zero rate is operator-actionable — the validator is shedding load. Resets on restart (modeled as gauge).
panels.title: Dropped Txs (cum.)

panels.description: Timeout-certificate rounds since hl-node start. View-change frequency — each TC round = a previous round timed out and the network had to advance via a TC. Sustained rate > 0 means the validator (or its peers) are unstable.
panels.title: TC Rounds (cum.)

panels.description: Catch-up events since hl-node start. Each one = the validator fell behind and had to recover from peers. Independent of TC rounds (you can catch up without timing out, and vice versa).
panels.title: Catch-up Events (cum.)

panels.description: Lifetime count of replay-from-checkpoint events observed via data/node_logs/replay/. A healthy validator has very few new entries per week.
panels.title: Replay Events (lifetime)

panels.description:
Per-validator block/tx/byte commit rate, derived from the cumulative accumulator gauges via deriv() — handles the restart resets cleanly (no spurious negative rates).

Y-axis: blocks/sec and txs/sec on the left, tx-bytes/sec on the right axis (separate scale). Flat lines that match the chain's expected cadence (testnet ≈ 5 blocks/sec) = healthy; dips = this validator lagging.
panels.title: Validator Commit Rate (blocks/txs/bytes per second)

panels.description:
Cumulative QC / TC / Catch-up counters since the hl-node process started. Y-axis = count (left axis for TC + Catchup, right axis for QC).

  • QC (right axis) — Quorum Certificates produced (= blocks with normal consensus). Tracks committed-block count almost 1:1 on a healthy network.
  • TC (left axis) — Timeout Certificates, i.e. rounds where consensus fell back to a view-change. Each TC line bump = one timeout. Healthy chains see <10 per day per validator.
  • Catchup (left axis) — events where this validator was behind and recovered from peers. Bumps = recovery events.

All three reset to 0 on hl-node restart (cumulative-since-source-start).
panels.title: QC / TC / Catch-up (cumulative since restart)

panels.description:
Live mempool depth (validator-only). Y-axis = item count per component:
  • committed_tx_hashes — distinct tx hashes the mempool tracks (often saturates at the configured cap, e.g. 100,000)
  • uncommitted_txs — live backlog: txs admitted but not yet in a block
  • blocks — pending blocks the mempool tracks (~1-5 healthy)
  • rpc_requests — active validator-RPC requests in flight

Sourced from periodic 'Size stats' events emitted every ~60s, so the panel updates at that cadence (not every scrape). committed_tx_hashes is on the right axis.
panels.title: Mempool Size by Component

panels.description: Mempool events per second by tag. add_tx + verify_block = arrival rate; committed = block commit rate; dropping_blocks = post-commit pruning of old block tracking (normal); size_stats fires every ~60s and emits the gauge values in the panel to the left.
panels.title: Mempool Events/sec

panels.description: add_tx + verify_block outcomes by status. 'ok' should dominate; anything else is a tx the mempool rejected.

targets.expr: sum by (event_type, status) (rate(hl_mempool_events_total{event_type=~"add_tx|verify_block", status!="", job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}[5m]))

panels.title: Mempool add_tx / verify_block Outcomes

panels.description: Validator-only consensus state machine latency by input type, p95. The TxCommit step is the validator-side block-commit timing — spikes here = the validator is slow to commit. HandleStateInput::Block = block-handling latency. WallClockBlockGap = how long between blocks measured locally.
panels.title: Consensus State Machine p95 Latency by Step

panels.description: Replay events: most recent height + timestamp. The age tells you 'how long since the validator last had to replay'. Healthy validators have ages in days, not minutes.
panels.title: Replay Event Summary

panels.description:
L1 block-apply phase latencies (validator-only). BeginBlock / DeliverSignedActions / EndBlock / RecoverUsers. The DeliverSignedActions step contains the actual tx-execution work — its p95 is the closest proxy to 'how long does each block's tx execution take'.

Source: data/latency_summaries/l1_task_latency/<step>/<YYYYMMDD>. If this panel shows no data, the node isn't currently writing those files — most common cause is a validator that hasn't proposed/executed L1 actions in the scrape window. Cross-check with `ls -la $NODE_HOME/data/latency_summaries/l1_task_latency/*/` on the node.
panels.title: L1 Task p95 Latency (BeginBlock / Deliver / EndBlock / Recover)

panels.description: Validator-RPC request flow. requests_registered = this validator served. requests_sent = this validator initiated. Roughly balanced on a healthy node.
panels.title: Validator RPC Requests (cum.)

panels.description: Operator-pushed configs that hl-node failed to load (counts files ending in _FAILED_LOAD in file_mod_time_tracker/). Any value > 0 = silent misconfiguration: the operator's intent is NOT being applied. This caught a stale firewall_ips.json_FAILED_LOAD on the test validator.
panels.title: Operator-Config FAILED_LOAD Files

panels.description: Combined stake held by the 3 largest validators as a percentage of total stake. A high value means a small cabal could threaten consensus.

targets.expr: sum(topk(3, max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}))) / sum(max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"})) * 100

panels.title: Top 3 Concentration

panels.description: Combined stake of the 10 largest validators as a percentage of total. > 50% means a coordinated top-10 could control consensus.

targets.expr: sum(topk(10, max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}))) / sum(max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"})) * 100

panels.title: Top 10 Concentration

panels.description: Combined stake of the 20 largest validators as a percentage of total. Useful as the upper bound — most validator-set decisions need 67%+, so a top-20 share over 80% means the long tail is decorative.

targets.expr: sum(topk(20, max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}))) / sum(max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"})) * 100

panels.title: Top 20 Concentration

panels.description: Number of validators each individually holding at least 1% of total stake. A rough decentralization proxy — more is better.
panels.title: Validators ≥ 1% Stake

panels.description: Each validator's stake as a percentage of total stake. Compare side-by-side with the Proposer Share panel to spot under- or over-performance.
panels.title: Stake Share % (top 30)

panels.description: Each validator's block-proposal count as a percentage of all blocks in the last 1h. Compare with the Stake Share panel: large mismatch = inefficient or jailed validator.

targets.expr: topk(30, (sum by (validator) (rate(hl_consensus_proposer_count_total{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}[1h])) / scalar(sum(rate(hl_consensus_proposer_count_total{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}[1h]))) * 100) * on(validator) group_left(name) (max by (validator, name) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) > bool 0))

panels.title: Proposer Share % over 1h (top 30)

panels.description: Top-30 validators' QC participation rate over time. Each row is one validator; each colored cell is their participation % at that minute. Red = missing rounds. Validators going dark show as red horizontal stripes — the cleanest 'who's missing turns' view there is.
panels.title: QC Participation Grid — top 30 validators

panels.description: hl-node and hl-visor up/down state over the dashboard time range. Green = up, red = down. Read it left-to-right as a rug of process health.
panels.title: Process Liveness Rug

panels.description: Each row is one hl-node subsystem's p95 latency over time. Colour gradient from cool (low latency) to warm (high). One look tells you which subsystem is hot right now and how persistent the heat is.
panels.title: Subsystem Latency Heatmap (p95)

panels.description:
Mempool events per second, stacked by event_type. Y-axis = events/sec. Bands from bottom up:
  • add_tx — transactions admitted into mempool
  • verify_block — block-payload verification calls
  • committed — blocks committed to mempool tracking
  • dropping_blocks — post-commit prune of old block tracking (normal)
  • size_stats — periodic capacity snapshots (~60s cadence)
Sustained narrow river = quiet chain; bands swelling together = load increase. An add_tx band shrinking while dropping_blocks grows = mempool admitting fewer txs while still pruning old ones (= submission slowdown).
panels.title: Mempool Activity River

panels.description:
Inbound P2P throughput per peer, stacked. Y-axis = raw per-30s normalized value from hl-node's tcp_traffic file — the units are NOT documented by hl-node, so treat as comparative (relative across peers, not in bytes/sec). Typical scale: ~1.0 for an active block-feeding peer, ~1e-7 for an idle peer.

Wider band = more bytes from that peer. The dominant (widest) band is the current 'parent peer'. Bands appearing/disappearing = peer churn (the node added or dropped that peer in the last 30s sample window).
panels.title: Inbound Peer Traffic Density (stacked)

panels.description: Stake concentration at a glance. Each slice is one of the top-15 validators by stake; the remainder is collapsed into 'rest'. The bigger the slice, the more network share that validator holds.
panels.title: Stake Distribution — top 15 + rest

panels.description:
Block-interval distribution heatmap. X = wall-clock time, Y = block-interval bucket in ms (hl-node's block_time_milliseconds histogram), colour = density (how many blocks fell in that bucket in the last 5-min window).

Reading it:
  • A tight bright horizontal band at one Y value = chain producing blocks at a consistent cadence. Healthy.
  • A second band higher up = there's a regular slow-block pattern (e.g. slow-state vs fast-state blocks have different intervals).
  • Vertical bright streaks reaching up to high Y = stall events (one block took multiple seconds).
  • Band widening over time = increasing jitter / variance.

Built from sum by(le) (rate(hl_core_block_time_milliseconds_bucket[5m])).
panels.title: Block Time Heatmap — chain rhythm

panels.description: Time since the most recent visor state sample. Green < 15s = healthy. Red > 60s = stalled, page someone. The dashboard's primary at-a-glance signal.
panels.title: Chain Liveness (visor sample age)

panels.description: Days until disk fills, extrapolated from the last 1h growth rate. Plan a cleanup below 30; urgent below 7.
panels.title: Days until disk full

panels.description: Wall-clock time since the latest core block timestamp. Independent of the visor sample. If this and Chain Liveness disagree, something's writing one path but not the other.
panels.title: Last core block — wall-clock age

panels.description: Any non-zero bug count is page-severity. Red whenever hl_node_bugs > 0.
panels.title: Bugs > 0

panels.description: Operator pushed config that hl-node rejected.
panels.title: FAILED_LOAD

panels.description: Filesystem under 10% free.
panels.title: Disk < 10% free

panels.description: Visor state file hasn't refreshed in > 60s.
panels.title: Stale visor sample

panels.description: More than 3 replay events in the last hour.
panels.title: Recovery storm

panels.description: hl-node restart count per day. Clusters → CDN failure storms (visor downloading new binary), OOM events, or operator-triggered restarts.
panels.title: Restart Timeline — hl-node runs per day
Validator Latency & RTT (per validator)
panels.description: Each row is one peer validator. RTT = TCP round-trip (network distance, microseconds). Latency EMA = consensus-loop processing latency this node observes for that peer's traffic (software-side). Sort by RTT to find geographically distant peers; sort by Latency to find peers whose software is slow.

How many validators fall into each stake-size bucket. X-axis = bucket (HYPE), Y-axis = count of validators in that bucket. Reveals the shape of the long tail — Top-N panels hide it; this surfaces it.

Buckets: <1k / 1k-10k / 10k-100k / 100k-1M / ≥1M HYPE. On Hyperliquid testnet (May 2026) the bulk of validators are in the 1k-10k range, with a small head above 1M and a long tail below 1k.

targets.expr: count((max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) / 1e8 >= 1000) and (max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) / 1e8 < 10000))

count((max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) / 1e8 >= 10000) and (max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) / 1e8 < 100000))

count((max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) / 1e8 >= 100000) and (max by (validator) (hl_consensus_validator_stake{job=~"$job", instance=~"$instance"}) / 1e8 < 1000000))

panels.title: Stake Distribution by Bucket

panels.description: add_tx events broken down by status. 'ok' should dominate; sustained non-ok rate = mempool throttling / dedup hits / invalid submissions.
panels.title: Mempool admission outcomes by status
Peers right now
panels.description: Unique peer IPs with positive traffic in the most recent tcp_traffic sample. Truly "right now": a peer only counts if it exchanged bytes with this node in the latest poll. Compare with the rolling-window panels below to see churn.

panels.title: Unique 24h
panels.description: Distinct peer IPs observed in the last 24h.

panels.title: Set fill (vs 2048 cap)
panels.description: How full the rolling 24h peer set is relative to the LRU cap. Computed from hl_p2p_unique_peers_seen{window="24h"} / 2048. Sustained near 100% means peers are getting evicted to make room (bump the cap if so).

panels.title: Evictions (cumulative)
panels.description: Peers evicted from the set since exporter start (LRU + TTL combined). Non-zero is normal if the node turns over many peers; rapid growth = peer-set thrashing.

panels.title: Unique peers by window
panels.description: Distinct peer IPs seen in rolling 5m / 1h / 24h windows. Long-tail = total reach, short window = currently-active set.

panels.title: New peers per minute
panels.description: Rate of new-peer registrations (hl_p2p_peers_added_total). High sustained rate without proportional growth in peers_total means the set is thrashing.

panels.title: Per-IP age (requires --per-peer-metrics)
panels.description: Seconds since each peer was last seen, one cell per IP. Requires --per-peer-metrics on the exporter. Empty when the flag is off.

title: Hyperliquid Exporter — All Metrics (v3)
